title: To...
content:
AFTER READING A LIFE AND LETTERS


ï¿½Cursed be he that moves my bones.ï¿½ 

Shakespeareï¿½s Epitaph.



You might have won the Poetï¿½s name,
If such be worth the winning now,
And gainï¿½d a laurel for your brow
Of sounder leaf than I can claim;

But you have made the wiser choice,
A life that moves to gracious ends
Throï¿½ troops of unrecording friends,
A deedful life, a silent voice.

And you have missï¿½d the irreverent doom
Of those that wear the Poetï¿½s crown;
Hereafter, neither knave nor clown
Shall hold their orgies at your tomb.

For now the Poet cannot die,
Nor leave his music as of old,
But round him ere he scarce be cold
Begins the scandal and the cry:

ï¿½Proclaim the faults he would not show;
Break lock and seal, betray the trust;
Keep nothing sacred, ï¿½tis but just
The many-headed beast should know.ï¿½

Ah, shameless! for he did but sing
A song that pleased us from its worth;
No public life was his on earth,
No blazonï¿½d statesman he, nor king.

He gave the people of his best;
His worst he kept, his best he gave.
My Shakespeareï¿½s curse on clown and knave
Who will not let his ashes rest!

Who make it seem more sweet to be
The little life of bank and brier,
The bird that pipes his lone desire
And dies unheard within his tree,

Than he that warbles long and loud
And drops at Gloryï¿½s temple-gates,
For whom the carrion vulture waits
To tear his heart before the crowd!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
